data.title: Absci: Downgrade To 'Hold' As ABS-101 On Back Burner And Pivot To ABS-201
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Absci Corporation (ABSI), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Absci: AI-Driven Platform Could Establish Best-In-Class TL1A Drug Targeting Profile." The hope here was that upon the release of data, the company could show a competing half-life when it came to its TL1A candidate ABS-101 in the targeting of patients with irritable bowel syndrome [IBS].

Today, I'm downgrading the stock to a Hold rating for several reasons. The first of which is that while ABS-101 showed an extended half-life versus 1st-generation TL1A candidates, it failed to make a mark in differentiation compared to 2nd-generation ones. Thus, the second reason for the downgrade is because the company has chosen to shift away from further development of ABS-101. The only way it intends to advance this forward is if it can find a partner willing to move it forward in clinical development [likely toward another indication, not IBD, as this landscape is crowded]. The third reason is because the company has chosen to pivot over to the development of prolactin receptor antagonist [anti-PRLR] ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with androgenetic alopecia [AA].

As a matter of fact, in the most recent update, it is noted that it had successfully dosed the first three dose cohorts in the single-ascending dose [SAD] portion of its ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial using ABS-201 to treat these androgenetic AA patients. While this program advancement appears to be good, there are a few problems here. The first of which is that it is early in the process. What I mean by that is that in the first half of 2026, it only expects to release the initial safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic [PK] profile of this study. The true test of whether or not this drug is effective in being able to treat these patients won't happen until proof-of-concept data is released in the 2nd half of 2026 and early 2027, respectively.

In addition, the company needs to obtain positive data from this phase 1/2a study in AA in order to move on to evaluating ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with endometriosis. Should this happen, then a phase 2 study for this candidate targeting endometriosis patients is expected to start in Q4 of 2026, and then the release of proof-of-concept data in the 2nd half of 2027.

The problem here is that privately held Hope Medicine has its own anti-PRLR candidate it is developing, known as HMI-115. The last time it reported data for the androgenetic AA patients was in 2024, and it has since pivoted over to starting a phase 3 study of HMI-115 treating patients with endometriosis. It is not clear why it shifted over to endometriosis, but this is what it has chosen for phase 3 development.

With the inability to differentiate ABS-101 as an anti-TL1A candidate for the treatment of patients with IBD, plus the shift over to anti-PRLR ABS-201 to AA, I believe it is important to downgrade this to a Hold rating. The reason for this rating is the small chance it somehow produces a superior candidate over Hope Medicine's HMI-115 candidate for either AA or endometriosis. Should it fail to have ABS-201 differentiate itself from HMI-115 in some way, then I would change the rating to a Sell.

ABS-201 For The Treatment Of Patients With Androgenetic Alopecia Areata Must Deliver In Huge Way

As I noted above, Absci Corporation failed to have its drug ABS-101 as an anti-TL1A candidate for the targeting of irritable bowel syndrome [IBS] disorders achieve an extended half-life over second-generation ones. With that in mind, plus the competitive landscape in IBD, it has chosen to put this candidate on an internal halt for further clinical development. In essence, it won't advance this candidate with its own cash but instead advance if it can find a partner that is willing to fund it. This is not going to be an easy task because the IBD field is crowded, and thus, unless it is able to find another suitable target indication to establish a partnership, this might not happen, if at all.

The whole point here is that the company has now shifted over to the development of anti-PRLR candidate ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with androgenetic alopecia [AA] in the ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. Specifically, Absci Corporation announced that it had completed the first three cohorts of the single-ascending dose [SAD] portion of this clinical trial. Thus far, the drug has been shown to be safe/tolerable. Before going over this clinical program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Androgenetic Alopecia [AA] is a genetic form of hair loss that occurs in both men and women. The driver that causes the hair loss for these patients is an androgen hormone by the name of dihydrotestosterone [DHT]. This is responsible for the miniaturization of hair follicles. Another name for this is set out for who it affects as well as follows:

  • For men, it could be known as male pattern baldness.
  • For females, it is said to be that of female pattern hair loss.

The global androgenetic alopecia areata market size is projected to reach $7.31 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity indeed. Consider that this is a very large unmet medical need. What makes me say that? Well, consider that in the United States alone there are 80 million Americans that live with androgenetic AA [Absci Corporation Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #17]. Not only that, but there hasn't been an FDA-approved drug for these patients since 1990. Furthermore, there needs to be improvement for therapies to not only slow down hair loss but also allow for hair regrowth.

Absci believes that ABS-201 might be able to achieve this, but this remains to be seen in clinical testing. The thing is that there are three stages in the cycle of hair growth, with prolactin [PRLR] being responsible for the first stage of Catagen + Apoptosis regression. They are:

  • Catagen + Apoptosis regression [phase 1].
  • Telogen resting phase [phase 2].
  • Anagen - active hair growth and new hair growth [phase 3].

The endgame goal is to use an anti-PRLR drug like ABS-201 to get rid of phases 1 & 2 and only go straight to phase 3 [the anagen hair regrowth and new growth phase].

In order to see whether or not ABS-201 as an anti-PRLR candidate is going to be able to help treat these patients with AA, it has already dosed the first patient in the ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. This early-stage, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study is expected to recruit up to a total of 227 patients both with and without AA. The reason why this is important is because this study is actually going to take a long time to complete. Why do I say that? That's because it has been established to be set as two different parts. There is a Single-Ascending Dose [SAD] portion of this study and then a Multiple-Ascending Dose [MAD] portion as well:

  • SAD portion of study - 32 healthy volunteers
  • MAD portion of study - 147 AGA patients.

The reason why it is important to bring this up is because the company is only currently at the SAD portion of this phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. The good news is that it has given an update that it has already dosed the first three cohorts of this trial. The first finding is that it has been well tolerated to date. To give you an idea, the SAD portion was laid out as follows:

  • Cohort 1 - 8 patients given a 150 mg intravenous [IV] dose of ABS-201
  • Cohort 2 - 8 patients given a 450 mg IV dose of ABS-201
  • Cohort 3 - 8 patients given a 900 mg IV dose of ABS-201
  • Cohort 4 - 8 patients given a 1800 mg IV dose of ABS-201.

The downside goes to what I noted directly above, which is that it is going to take some time to run this clinical study. Consider that Absci believes that it can release initial data from the SAD portion of this phase 1/2a trial in the 1st half of 2026. The problem with this is that this only deals with the healthy volunteers, and it is only to show initial safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic [PK] profile data.

The point I'm getting at here is that in order to see if the drug truly has an effect on AA, it needs to release proof-of-concept data from the MAD portion of this study. This is going to be broken down into interim 13-week POC data in the 2nd half of 2026 and then 26-week top-line data in early 2027.

One treatment that boosts the anagen [hair regrowth] phase would be minoxidil. What Absci is trying to do is to be able to enhance what has already been approved. The first item is to offer up an anti-PRLR therapy like ABS-201 with fewer side effects. Secondly, one that is potentially far more durable. The problem with minoxidil is that it has to be taken constantly, as if not taken, hair loss will resume. Lastly, potentially offer better hair regrowth. Thus, ABS-201 must not only do better than currently available products like minoxidil but must be able to compete with HMI-115 as well from Hope Medicine.

The good news is that in terms of showing improvement over minoxidil, it is on the right track when it comes to preclinical testing. In a 21-day hair regrowth model, ABS-201 showed superior efficacy over 5% topical minoxidil [Absci Corporation Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #22]. Lastly, based on non-human primate [NHP] testing, it is believed that the half-life could allow the company to dose humans once every 8 weeks [Q8W] or once every 12 weeks [Q12W]. However, there is no assurance of this, and it remains to be seen if it actually translates into humans.

I believe that this program has a good shot, but it goes back to what I stated above in the beginning. Despite Hope Medicine seeing some decent phase 2 data for its study, having shown a statistically significant mean non-vellus target area hair count [TAHC] increase of 14 hairs/cm^2 from baseline, it still chose to move over to an extensive endometriosis program. There has been no update on its AA program since that I could find in the press releases of Hope Medicine.

This brings me to the next point, which is that Absci is only to move on to initiating a phase 2 study using ABS-201 to treat patients with endometriosis if and only if it observes positive data from its phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. This means if it misses the mark on efficacy or runs into safety issues, then it would not only set back the androgenetic AA program but also the advancement of this anti-PRLR candidate for endometriosis as well.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Absci Corporation had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $144.3 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company had to raise a lot of cash throughout 2025. As a matter of fact, it tapped into two different ATM agreements. One was an ATM agreement known as a "Prior Sales Agreement" made with Cowen and Company, where the company could offer and sell up to an aggregate of $100 million worth of common shares of stock. For the year ending December 31, 2025, it issued 10,377,752 shares and generated $35.7 million in net proceeds.

Then, it tapped into another ATM transaction in 2025 as well. This was in terms of an at-the-market offering agreement with TD Securities LLC back in August of 2025. With this agreement, the aggregate amount it could issue and sell was also set to be $100 million worth of common stock. For the year ending December 31, 2025, it issued 927,855 shares and generated $3.5 million in net proceeds under this. That's not all; it had also enacted a big public offering raise back on July 28, 2025, with several underwriters. It sold a total of 16,670,000 shares of its common stock to raise net proceeds of $46.7 million after deducting expenses.

Lastly, in January of 2025, it enacted a strategic deal with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The beauty of this deal was that AMD injected $20 million as an investment into Absci through a private investment in public equity [PIPE] agreement. The company has plenty of cash on hand, and I don't believe that there is the risk of any near-term dilution at all. What makes me say this? Well, the mere fact that it has given guidance that it believes it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2028.

The company burns roughly $116.5 million of cash per year. This is broken down into $81.4 million in R&D expenses and then $35.1 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Absci Corporation. The first risk to consider would be in terms of its anti-TL1A candidate not being able to meet the bar in terms of an extended half-life over second-generation candidates. This was a major roadblock, and the company had decided not to pursue the development of ABS-101 for the treatment of IBD on its own. With that being said, it intends to find a partner that is willing to advance this candidate for either IBD or any other indication.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company will be able to find a partner that is willing to fund this candidate any further. Especially since it didn't meet the bar in terms of extending the half-life to be better than that of second-generation anti-TL1A candidates. Nor that it will eventually be able to further advance this clinical candidate into additional testing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of anti-PRLR candidate ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with androgenetic alopecia in the ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE study. As I have noted above, the data to be released in the 1st half of 2026 is only set to be that of initial safety, tolerability, and PK profile data from healthy volunteers.

The risk here is that, first and foremost, there is no guarantee that there will be no safety issues found relating to this drug. Secondly, even though Hope Medicine with HMI-115 has been able to show the drug to work well in 12 men, there is no guarantee that ABS-201 is going to have a similar or superior clinical outcome compared to it.

Speaking of which, this is another risk to consider here with ABS-201, which is that not only must this anti-PRLR candidate show to have superior efficacy to drugs like minoxidil, but it must also appear to have a similar or equivalent profile to that of HMI-115. Plus, without achieving positive data from the phase 1/2a HEADLINE study of ABS-201 in targeting AA patients, the company is not going to be able to initiate the potential phase 2 endometriosis program.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of a novel AI-designed anti-HER2 antibody for the treatment of patients with cancer. This candidate, known as ABS-501, has been able to show increased or equivalent affinity to Herceptin [trastuzumab] in preclinical models.

The risk here is that this candidate is still in the process of being developed in pre-IND-enabling studies. The point here is that there is no assurance that Absci will be able to achieve positive pre-IND enabling studies in order to move this candidate forward into phase 1 clinical testing. Nor that ABS-501 is going to be effective in being able to treat HER2-expressing solid tumors in phase 1 and beyond.

Conclusion

Absci Corporation had a major problem relating to the development of anti-TL1A candidate ABS-101 for the treatment of patients with IBD. It was able to get its half-life extension technology in place to overcome 1st-generation candidates. However, with so much competition in place [2nd-generation anti-TL1A candidate], it wasn't able to create a differentiated product worthy of continuing development. That's why it had decided not to spend its own cash on further development of ABS-101 for the treatment of these patients. Plus, it believes that it has a better shot at moving forward with its other candidate, ABS-201, for the treatment of patients with AA and endometriosis.

As I have shown above, this biotech has a steep hill to climb. That's because Hope Medicine moved on straight to a phase 3 program for endometriosis, and not much has been shown since releasing initial phase 2 data of HMI-115 for androgenetic AA. Thus, my conclusion for now is to place a Hold rating on this stock.

Why? Perhaps the company can somehow pull off an extended half-life for ABS-201 in the targeting of patients with either AA or endometriosis. Everything for this company now hinges on if it can deliver solid data from the phase 1/2a HEADLINE study targeting androgenetic AA patients. If it doesn't, then that will set back this company even further. At such a point, I might have to reevaluate the rating of ABSI stock as well.

data.title: Thoughts On The Dollar
data.description:
The dollar's resilience since the war began is not a mystery, but it is being misread. The Dollar Index (DXY) has appreciated roughly 1.8% this month alone, following a modest 0.65% gain in February after January's 1.35% slide. Against every G10 currency, the greenback has advanced since hostilities commenced. All but the Canadian dollar and sterling have surrendered more than 1.5%. The move looks like strength. It is, in part. But it is also a story about positioning, leverage, and the jump in US rates.

Unpacking Safe Haven

Two channels are driving the dollar's gains. The first is mechanical: short covering. For months, traders had used the dollar as a funding currency; borrowing cheap in greenbacks to reach for yield in Latin American bonds, for example, and other higher-beta assets. When those risk positions went south, and they invariably do, they were sold, and the funding currency was bought back, which gives the appearance of safe-haven demand.

Another layer of the same dynamic came from foreign equity investors who had been financing the US current account deficit, and many of them with dollar hedges layered on top. As US stocks tumbled, the shares were liquidated and the short-dollar hedges were lifted, or, for some, the drop in equity values left them simply over-hedged, and they trimmed accordingly. Either way, the bid for dollars was structural, not discretionary.

The second channel is more recognizable as genuine safe-haven demand. War compresses risk appetite, and with the United States now the world's largest energy producer, some investors judge the American economy as comparatively insulated from the oil shock radiating out of the Gulf. That is a reasonable, if incomplete, argument. It is also the kind of narrative that tends to have a shorter shelf life than the positioning adjustment does.

US Rates more than Differentials in the First Instance

What is less appreciated is how sharply the rates landscape has shifted and how that is influencing the dollar in ways that go beyond the usual interest rate differential logic. Since the war began at the end of last month, the two-year Treasury yield has risen roughly 53 basis points. The ten-year yield is up about 45 bp. While theory says interest rate differentials are important, in the short run the jump in US rates seems to offer a better explanation of the dollar’s strength. Perhaps when rates spike on supply shocks and inflation fears rather than growth optimism, it changes the dynamics.

And then there is the Federal Reserve. Before the war, fed funds futures had fully priced two cuts this year and assigned roughly 40% odds to a third. By the time the FOMC met last week, some of that easing had been walked back, but the market still expected at least one cut. The statement moved the needle a little. It was Chair Powell's framing in the press conference that did the heavy lifting. He played down the seemingly incongruous projections, which showed the median view having increased the GDP forecast and growth forecasts while keeping the unemployment rate steady and repeating the one rate this year that was seen in December 2025.

Powell was also unusually candid about the Fed's limitations. He acknowledged that missing the inflation target is more the norm than the exception in recent years and that the war poses genuine challenges for both mandates simultaneously.

Higher Prices Bite

Meanwhile, at the gas pump, the political economy is shifting in real time. Retail gasoline prices have risen every single day since the war began — weekends included — adding roughly a dollar per gallon to bring the national average close to $4.00. Grocery prices appear to be following. This is the kind of inflation that is felt viscerally, that does not require an economics degree to understand, and that generates political pressure with a speed that quarterly GDP estimate does not.

Against this backdrop, the Fed's framing of the labor market deserves scrutiny. Powell's characterization of "recent stability" downplayed the loss of 92,000 jobs in February. He acknowledged, correctly, that the administration's immigration policies have weighed on labor force growth. That is the third policy (joining the tariffs and war) that is deterring this Fed and the next chair from being able to deliver the lower rates that the president has incessantly and angrily demanded.

Powell made it clear that he intends to serve as chair until his successor is confirmed by the Senate, which will not go forward until the investigation is over. He also indicated he would not relinquish his governor seat (term runs into 2028) while the investigation is ongoing in any fashion. This is neither unprecedented nor common. He sees it as a defense of institutional independence. Markets should take him at his word.

DXY

Technically, the Dollar Index peaked for the year on March 13 near 100.50. Since then, it has found support just below 99.00, hovering above the 20-day moving average, which now sits a fraction above that level. The index has spent little time above 100 since mid-last year, and the daily momentum indicators are rolling over from overbought territory. That argues for some consolidation. But speculative positioning, though it has shifted dramatically — net long euro positions collapsed from around 150,000 contracts to barely 20,000, and the market has swung to nearly 70,000 contracts net short yen — does not yet look extreme enough to make a compelling case that the adjustment is finished, especially with the fear the war escalates wafting in the air.

The war premium in the dollar is real. So is the rate support. But both are contingent on an escalation path that remains deeply uncertain. The five-day pause announced this week is being viewed skeptically, and rightly so. There still seems to be a risk that the war escalates. The US is moving more troops to the area, and this is spurring speculation that the US could take Kharg Island, a critical export facility for Iranian oil.

Until there is more clarity, the greenback will retain a bid. When clarity comes, the unwind could be swift. We have seen a couple of such responses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Huang, Zuckerberg, Ellison Join Trump's Tech Council
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The president could name up to 24 advisors on new tech panel. (0:15) Memory chip stocks fall after Google unveils more efficient AI algorithms. (1:22) BlackRock CEO Larry Fink tells private credit investors to ‘live with it.’ (1:59)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Out top story so far, President Donald Trump has appointed some of the biggest names in business a new President’s Council of Advisors on Science and Technology.

The appointees include Meta Platforms (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Oracle (ORCL) Executive Chairman Larry Ellison and Nvidia' (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) CEO Lisa Su and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) co-founder Sergey Brin will also serve.

The White House says the panel will focus on topics related to the opportunities and challenges that emerging technologies present to the American workforce and ensure all Americans thrive in the Golden Age of Innovation.

It will be co-chaired by David Sacks, who has served as White House AI and crypto czar, and Michael Kratsios, another technology advisor.

Thirteen members have been appointed so far and the total is expected to rise to 24.

"We’re delighted to see NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang appointed to this council and to have the opportunity to advance American leadership in AI," the chipmaker posted. "America’s strength in science and technology depends on a vibrant ecosystem of researchers, developers, and industry working together to drive innovation and global leadership."

Among active stocks, memory stocks are down after Google revealed a set of new algorithms designed to reduce the amount of memory needed to run large language models and vector search engines.

Micron (MU), Western Digital (WDC), Seagate Technology (STX) and Sandisk (SNDK) were all under pressure.

Chewy (CHWY) missed estimates with its fourth-quarter earnings report and set mixed guidance. But shares are sharply higher as investors eyed the confident tone from the company that stressed it is in a position of real strength with expanding margins and strong cash generation.

And Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) is surging after the company posted initial data from Phase 1/2 trials for its experimental drugs targeted at two rare forms of muscular dystrophies.

And in other news of note, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink has some blunt advice for investors frustrated by limited redemptions in private credit funds: deal.

“On the front page of the contract… you’re investing in something that has less liquidity,” he told the BBC’s “Big Boss Interview” podcast. “You have to accept the notion that redemptions are gonna be limited to 5% a quarter.”

“Those are the rules. Live with it.”

In addition, he stressed that unlike the Financial Crisis, private credit does not pose a systemic risk to markets, despite recent redemption pressures on some retail funds.

The Financial Crisis in in 2007 “was based on hidden leverage, just gigantic leverage balance sheets,” he said. “This is not a leverage balance sheet problem.”

While some retail investors are seeking to exit, Fink said institutional demand remains strong. “More people are trying to get in,” he said, adding that “we actually had more subscriptions than redemptions” in the HLEND fund.

And the senior leaders of Meta Platforms (META) stand to earn hundreds of millions of dollars if they help the company reach a $9T valuation in the next five years.

The company awarded new stock options to its seven main executives, excluding CEO Zuckerberg, which are exercisable only if the company's stock price surges at least 88% by 2031, according to filing. Senior execs could enjoy the full benefit of Meta's stock options program if the company's share price jumps more than 500% within the provided time frame.

data.title: Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The fourth quarter of 2025 saw a continuation of themes from earlier in the year. Equity markets continued to rally, with the S&P 500® Index reaching new all-time highs in December. This rally was especially prominent in international equities, which outperformed U.S. equities over the quarter.

In the U.S., a September interest rate cut was followed by additional cuts in October and December, but the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) was divided on this approach, due to competing pressures from still-elevated inflation and economic data. However, these rate cuts did not affect yields dramatically as they were already priced-in. The U.S. dollar remained relatively stable, while commodities were more volatile. Gold prices continued to reach new highs, but this quarter also saw a dramatic increase in prices for silver, platinum, palladium, and copper. Energy prices, on the other hand, decreased going into year-end.

Performance

The Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Class I (ASFYX, Fund) shares returned 4.09% during the fourth quarter of 2025, while the Fund's benchmark, the Credit Suisse Managed Futures Liquid Index, returned 3.85% for the quarter. It is important to note that there are material differences between the Fund and its benchmark. Another index that may be used for performance analysis is the SG Trend Index as it reflects a peer group of diversified, primarily trend-following investment managers. This benchmark returned 4.86% during the fourth quarter of 2025.

The Fund maintained broad diversification across trend horizons and approaches to trend following in the fourth quarter. The Fund saw gains from its long equity positions and commodities (especially precious metals); currencies also provided some small gains. Fixed income detracted from performance.

Equities continued to climb over the quarter, leading to gains from the Fund's long positions, particularly within international developed markets. The largest gains came from Japanese equities, the S&P/TSX 60 (Canada), and the IBEX 35 (Spain).

In commodities, the largest gains came from long positions in precious metals, especially silver and gold. Base metals, particularly copper and aluminum, also provided gains. These gains offset losses from energies and agricultural commodities. Energy losses came primarily from long positions, despite gains from a long position in EU carbon emissions. Agricultural losses were driven by soymeal and soybeans.

Currency gains came from short positions in the Japanese yen (long USD) and from long positions in emerging currencies like the South African rand and Mexican peso (short USD).

In fixed income, long positions in international and U.S. bonds detracted from performance as bond prices fell over the quarter; the strategy did have gains from short positions in Australian bonds.

At the model level, all three approaches contributed positively to performance, with the adaptive and basic multi-trend models outperforming the short-horizon approaches. Within the portion of the portfolio allocated to short-term, high-quality securities (the money market portion), short-term rates at current levels continued to positively impact performance.

Risk Management

The portfolio is managed to an annualized volatility target of 17% or less. 1 We scale portfolio positions as often as daily in an effort to keep total portfolio risk within its target range. As market volatility increases, position sizes are reduced, and as market volatility decreases, position sizes are increased. The Fund's realized volatility during the quarter was 13.52%, higher than the long-term realized volatility for the Fund. We continue to scale the size of the Fund's positions to keep total portfolio risk at or below its target.

Outlook

2026 began with strong upward momentum in global equity markets. In the coming quarter, we anticipate that markets will continue to monitor the balance between growth, inflation, and policy. The macro landscape has become more fragmented across regions in its approach to monetary policy. Going into 2026, it has the potential for both booms and busts, suggesting a wide range of potential outcomes.

For the United States, there is a potential for overstimulation from macro policies like tax cuts, and from the potential for additional rate cuts, which will be affected by the composition and independence of the Fed. There is also a risk, given high valuations and the potential for weakening economic conditions, that rate cuts may provide little support. All the while, geopolitical concerns continue to be front-of-mind for many investors. In addition to the conflicts we have been watching unfold over previous years, the U.S. invasion of Venezuela may impact oil prices and other global policies.

1 The Fund seeks to target a relatively stable level of annualized volatility (as measured by standard deviation), but the targeted volatility is subject to change. There is no guarantee that the target will be achieved and the realized volatility level of the Fund can be higher or lower than its target volatility at any given point in time. Volatility is not an indicator of expected return or a measure of protection against loss.

data.title: Why I'm Not Waiting Any Longer To Double Down On Gold
data.description:
Is the bull market in gold over?

Is the bull market in gold (GLD, XAUUSD:CUR) finally over? Nah… don't buy into that absurdity if anyone were to tell you that. Thankfully, based on the most recent research and commentaries that I have parsed and looked into carefully, no one is telling me that the bull market is over. Yet, that doesn't mean that market forecasters or prognosticators have not tried looking into their crystal ball to try and make their guesstimates into why gold suffered a surprising selloff in the midst of the Iranian War.

It was only in January that I highlighted that the bullish thesis in gold was only warming up, but the bear market decline since then has threatened to upend that narrative. So we are given another opportunity to take a closer look at why this has happened, and what it portends from this moment on, and also whether it still represents a great buying opportunity for those of us who have yet to get on board the gold glitter.

As for me, I'm seeing a fantastic opportunity, but first, I know we have to go to some of the key reasons that you may have read about on why this bullish gold train formed a top back in January, before dropping into a rare bear market, as we can see from above.

I'm observing that a bullish reversal signal is already underway this week, aligning very nicely with the possible negotiations taking place right now between the U.S. and Iran about putting an end to the current hostilities. Of course, it's still too early for us to confirm whether it's a temporary ceasefire, or something "more permanent," so to speak.

Nevertheless, I always believe that we must pay homage to price action signals, because they represent what investors are telling us right now as they rejected further selling that initially caused gold to collapse into a bear market this month.

From the price action perspective, this is just about the "near-perfect" signal I to add positions to the yellow glittering metal. For those of you who can read tea leaves (price action), I believe you should be able to observe the bullish print after what appears to be a very nice M-shaped signal that typically precedes solid bottoms alongside an up-trending orientation. Another important observation: gold price action also nearly tested the 50-week moving average (blue line), and getting an affirmative reaction from the dip buyers at the current juncture only serves to improve the underlying strength of the signal, in my opinion.

A structurally bullish gold market remains the base case

For those of you who pay close attention to commentary regarding what has caused the recent gold rally to sputter and then drop into a bear market, I urge you to filter the signal from the noise.

Reasons presented include investors unloading after a crazy run in 2025, or retail investors facing liquidity events due to losses in other asset classes, tapping into their gold exposure to cover these needs.

I have also read about countries that are importing oil that may need to use part of their gold reserves to fund the significantly increased energy costs due to the current near shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz.

I’ve also gleaned commentary highlighting that the market is now pricing in a more hawkish Federal Reserve, if the current conflict in the Middle East does not get resolved expeditiously, resulting in possible interest rate hikes, and thus creating headwinds for gold investors, as gold doesn’t pay interest/income on the holdings. In a sense, investors are less willing to hold gold, as they have other asset classes that give them supposedly higher income returns.

Well, let me just say, good try nonetheless. I will now dissect some of these views, and give you my vantage point on why, while they seem intelligently crafted and with good intentions to help us make sense of the dilemma (gold crashing in heightened geopolitical conflicts), they don't quite matter when we consider the structurally bullish narrative in gold in the long run.

Okay, the market now thinks that rate cuts are no longer the base case when we refer to the latest revisions to the federal funds target rate outlook. But it's not like we are expecting the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates like what they did in 2022, right? It's probably just a temporary pause, allowing more time for the central bank to take a look at the inflationary dynamics, which they need to juxtapose against how the unemployment situation is working out, to come to a more balanced view on monetary policy.

However, what do you think of the state of the gold holdings as a ratio of total reserves? It is still going up, isn't it, and we are not even close to historical levels, as the world starts to diversify more of their holdings towards gold.

While we could point out that after the start of the Russia-Ukraine war, the reallocation to gold as a ratio of total reserves surged, the trend had started even before that, as you can glean from the chart. So the question about whether these reallocations can continue to go higher beyond the 20% mark to >30% mark is something that we need to let play out.

Therefore, even though the Fed funds rate has come down significantly from the 5.3% level back in 2023, it has not stopped investors from stepping up their purchases of gold, as central banks around the world seek to diversify from their dollar-based holdings.

U.S. government debt helps the bullish gold narrative

Next, I think we have gotten an insightful look into how the federal government debt will balloon through the 2050s if we don't take decisive actions to curb our spending profligacy at the current state.

At the same time, it also stunned me when I read about the Pentagon's request for an additional $200B to fund the ongoing Iranian War. Those aren't immaterial sums, mind you. And questions about whether the U.S. could continue to fight these conflicts in the Middle East or even other parts of the world, given the current fiscal situation. It really doesn't help matters, and likely spurs investors to reassess whether it makes sense to hedge with gold even more.

So, unless the U.S. benefits from a significant productivity boom in AI (raising GDP growth markedly), helping them to lower the debt and change the trajectory decisively, the market is not likely to bet on it.

Sure, the current AI boom is benefiting the infrastructure builders, enlightening us with the so-called HALO trade, or heavy assets low obsolescence, as it is commonly known. A very nice expression informing us that investing in companies with high capital expenditure benefiting from the AI boom is the current zeitgeist in this unprecedented AI era.

In contrast, if we had put our money in the battered software stocks, with low capital expenditure and supposedly very high gross margins, that may not be sustainable, as the market now wonders whether that terminal value from their long duration cash flows could be whittled down to nothing over time, as they face potentially damaging disruption themes, maybe not imminent yet, but still debilitating over a period of time.

Hence, for investors, unless the federal deficit trajectory changes for the U.S. due to incredible growth in GDP attributed to a formidable AI monetization experience, something must change (lowering debt/spending?). Also, who knows whether the AI boom could also cause widespread AI related unemployment, and curtail consumer spending, hurting the main engine for the U.S. economy?

In this case, can we blame these investors as they navigate these uncharted waters and continue betting on gold as protection against these ambiguities? And with the world becoming an even more dangerous place, with also worried about the U.S. potentially weaponizing its currency to fulfill its own (self-interested) objectives, keeping too much assets in USD may seem too aggressive, and potentially perilous.

It is with this context that I believe why investors are more than willing to come back to support gold right now, after seeing the price action decline into a bear market, which I doubt it will last too long.

So consider taking full advantage while the opportunity is still timely, and while the momentum swing traders are still nursing their wounds, and have not returned yet.

data.title: WW International Is Great For Losing Weight And Losing Money For Shareholders
data.description:
Shareholders of WW International (WW) lost money as the company, the former Weight Watchers International. Inc, slid into bankruptcy last year and continued to see the new equity issued under the Ch.11 reorganization plan decline as the company restructured their business model. For a number of reasons that will be covered in this article, including too much expensive debt, WW stock remains a sell. This is an update to my prior WW articles.

Bankruptcy Reorganization Plan Was Irrational

WW International filed for Ch.11 bankruptcy, which I expected, on May 6, 2025 and exited bankruptcy on June 24. Under the reorganization plan (docket 18), WW shareholders were "gifted" a token recovery of 0.01073733 new WW share for each old WW share held. (Gifting often makes the bankruptcy process faster and less likely to have lawsuits filed by shareholders.) Based on the current WW price of $17.60, this recovery is currently worth about $0.19 per old (WGHTQ) share.

I think, however, that the reorganization plan was completely irrational because it resulted in WW being highly leveraged. First lien claim holders received 91% of the new stock and $465 million term loans with an interest rate of SOFR+6.80 for their $1.62 billion claim. The rate on this term loan was 10.49% as of 12/31/25.

Instead of having $465 million very expensive debt, WW could currently have no long-term debt if the first lien claim holders just received stock. The value of the stock received would, in theory, be worth more by not having the debt because the enterprise value remains the same. Not only are they now highly leveraged, they are paying almost $50 million annually for interest expenses. They could use this cash to expand their business instead of paying debt holders.

Over the last few years I constantly see irrational reorganization plans being confirmed by the courts which years ago "old school" bankruptcy judges would have rejected. For example, Spirit Airlines reorganization plan was irrational, which I covered, and they ended up back in bankruptcy court.

What Happened With Intangible Asset Values?

Their post-bankruptcy balance sheet using "fresh start accounting" is a complete shock. While goodwill declined, which was expected, from $239.593 million at the end of 2024 to $200.135 million at the end of 2025, net other intangible assets increased dramatically to $490.664 million at the end of 2025 from $115.762 million at the end of 2024.

Year-End Balance Sheet 2025 and 2024

This $374.902 million increase in other intangible assets is greater than the $318.071 million year-end 2025 total shareholder equity. Without this increase, shareholder equity would be a deficit of $56.831 million.

Determination of Net Other Intangible Assets 2025

Determination of Net Intangible Assets 2024

The primary reason for the increase was the $320 million for trade name. I am sorry, but I have serious doubts that their trade names are now worth $320 million. According to their 10-K this number is based on:

trade names valued under the relief from royalty income approach, the royalty rate was estimated to be 6.0%. For the developed technology-based intangibles that were valued using the relief from royalty income approach, the royalty rate was estimated to be 4.0% for Behavioral developed technology and 5.0% for Clinical developed technology

If future revenue does not significantly increase over an extended period of time, there could, in my opinion, be non-cash charges on future income statements to reflect a reduction in the carrying values of intangible assets.

Business Model Still Relies on Membership Subscriptions

WW International's business model still is based on membership subscriptions. This membership model, in my opinion, might have been appropriate when Oprah Winfrey was associated with Weight Watchers and there was a sense of belonging to a group for moral support, while trying to reduce weight via strict diets and exercise. Before they were not really selling a specific product - it was more of a service. Now they are selling specific products, including Wegovy which the FDA approved last December, and attempting to add on support services via their membership subscription program.

They are moving away from their traditional Weight Watchers behavioral subscription model, which they expect to drop 26% in 1Q '26 from 1Q '25, to clinical subscriptions, which they expect to increase to 200,000 at the end of 1Q '26. That is a sharp increase from 130,000 at the end of 2025. An aggressive and expensive marketing campaign in 1Q is a major factor behind this growth.

Membership is not cheap. According to their website:

Offer ends 3/31/26 at 11:59 PM PDT. Pay $25/month for your first two months with a 12-month WW Med+ plan purchase. After the first two months, membership continues for $74 per month for the remainder of your 12-month plan. Programs do not include the cost of GLP-1 medications. All medications require eligibility and a prescription. Plan auto-renews for another 12-month period at the same monthly rate at the end of each plan term unless canceled.

The $74/month does not include the actual cost for GLP-1. With recent soaring gasoline prices I can see why WW stock is dropping. Some people on tight budgets can't afford $74/month.

According to CEO Tara Comonte during the recent conference call "we're about so much more than just a prescription". OK, but is the "more" actually worth $74/month? This is what members get according to their website:

  • Meet with a Registered Dietitian for easier, tailored meal planning

  • Use your camera to track meals and their Points® value

  • 350+ ZeroPoint, no-track foods

  • Automatic macro tracking of any food you eat

  • Generate Points® value totals for any recipe on the internet

  • Access to more affordable weight-loss meds

Their potential growth is huge because according to the CDC 41.9% of adults over 20 are obese (obesity is defined as having a body mass index of over 30). I just think that many potential customers would just decide to buy GLP-1. drugs directly from a discount pharmacy and avoid paying $888 for year's membership. Taking GLP-1 is, in theory, the easy way to reduce weight. Why complicate this easy process with some expensive support subscription?

Valuation Based on Guidance Numbers

Management is expecting revenue of $620 - $635 million in 2026 with EBITDA of $105-$115 million.

This implies an enterprise value/EBITDA multiple of 4.18x - 4.58x. (Total long-term debt of $465 million minus cash of $160 million, plus equity capitalization of $176 million= $481 million enterprise value based on WW stock price of $17.60) These may seem like low multiples, but they are not, in my opinion.

First, you can not ignore "I" - interest expenses, which are almost $50 million annually. Second, the company is highly leveraged compared to the quality of balance sheet asset values. Third, since many consumers are being squeezed by higher gas prices, it might be difficult to achieve their EBITDA guidance numbers. Next, there is a high risk that their business model transformation will be less successful than WW bulls expect.

Conclusion

You would think that Weight Watchers should be booming since the number of obese adults has soared over the last few decades to almost 42% of the population. The problem is their membership subscription model - it is not cheap. I have serious doubts they can grow their clinical subscriptions to a level that will generate large profits.

WW International missed a chance in bankruptcy to become free of all long-term debt. Now they are highly leveraged with a weak balance sheet that has inflated intangible assets. At this point I still consider WW stock a sell.

data.title: Circle Falls As Stablecoin Future Questioned
data.description:
Tuesday was a very bad day for shareholders of Circle Internet Group, Inc. (CRCL). The company behind the USDC stablecoin saw its shares drop more than 20%, the worst trading day percentage wise since the company went public. The culprit seemingly was some potentially bad news on legislation regarding the Clarity Act, which could limit the future potential of the stablecoin space.

Previous coverage of the name

My most recent article on Circle came back in January, at which point I detailed a couple of major headwinds the company was facing. With U.S. interest rates dropping and USDC in circulation pulling back, it appeared that Circle's Q1 street revenue estimates were too high. Shares have rallied back more than 36% since, despite the S&P 500 (SP500) being down more than 5% over that time. However, Circle still remains well below its all-time high of just under $299 that was reached shortly after going public.

Recent legislative news

When President Trump was re-elected in 2024, the entire crypto industry celebrated as they believed he would be a major supporter of the space. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) surged to a new all-time high, stablecoins like USDC saw tremendous growth in circulation, and it seemed like nothing was impossible. Last year, a crypto winter started with prices falling, and the future of the industry suddenly didn't look as bright.

Earlier this week, some proposed new legislation regarding stablecoins seemingly put things into question for companies like Circle. The latest draft of the Clarity Act would ban stablecoin issuers from paying yield to customers just for holding the assets. Earning yields on balances, otherwise known as rewards, is an incentive for holding the coins, basically like getting interest on your deposits at a bank.

The latest part of the bill would potentially still allow for yields to be generated by using the stablecoin for payments, trading, or lending it out. In recent years, major banks have been opponents of stablecoin yields, as they are seen as a potential competitor. At a minimum, the latest news out of D.C. at least provides more uncertainty for the future of stablecoins, and it is not clear when and if final legislation will make it up for a vote.

An update on USDC growth

As a reminder, Circle generates a majority of its revenue from interest on the reserves that back the USDC stablecoin. 2025 was a huge year for USDC in circulation growth, surging by more than $31 billion. While U.S. interest rates declined, Circle was still able to report record revenue in Q4 that handily beat street estimates, rising 77% over the prior year period.

After topping out in mid-Q4 at around $78.5 billion, USDC in circulation took a hit as crypto prices fell. The number fell to around $70 billion earlier this year, but it has risen back towards new highs during March. Circle in most of its recent quarters had generated interest on its reserves that comes in almost 20 basis points below SOFR, which means in Q1 should be a net return of about 3.50% on an annualized basis.

While there is still some time left in the quarter, let's assume that USDC in circulation averaged about $75 billion for the quarter. That would mean about $656 million in interest revenue for Circle, and other revenues in the period could be around $40 million as management has guided to full year other revenues of $150 million to $170 million for the year. In the chart below, you can see how Street estimates for Q1 have trended over time.

Currently at $719 million, I still think the Street is a little too high for Q1 at the moment. There's still plenty of time for numbers to change, however, as we don't yet know how much USDC in circulation there will be at the end of the quarter. Perhaps the good news for Circle on the interest rate front is that expectations no longer call for multiple rate cuts from the Fed this year, which would eat further into the return rate that the company generates.

The current valuation picture

Circle trades at more than 91 times its expected adjusted earnings per share as of midday Wednesday. That's still well above the more than 48 times that Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) goes for. Both firms are expected to see their adjusted earnings decline this year, and a lot more so for Circle. However, Circle is expected to show more than 13% revenue growth this year, while Coinbase is projected for just 1.25%. On a price-to-sales basis, Circle has again flipped to being more expensive, going for 8.10 times this year's projected revenue, with Coinbase around 6.65 times.

When it comes to Street analysts, the picture is very bullish currently. Of the 23 analysts covering the stock, 9 have Strong Buy ratings, with 2 more having Buy ratings. There are 11 analysts with Hold ratings and 1 with a Strong Sell. The average price target is nearly $128, implying more than 22% upside from current levels. I should note, however, that the Street average has come down by $12 since my previous article, and it was $235 in the stock's early trading days, so analysts have reduced their valuations a bit over time.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Circle had its worst public trading day yet on Tuesday, as shares fell more than 20% due to potential legislative headwinds. There now seems to be an effort in Congress to limit stablecoin yields, potentially putting a major roadblock in front of the future of this emerging business. With the big banks trying to fight off this evolving financial technology, it remains to be seen what the final legislation will end up being.

Until we get a better idea of how this will all turn out, I am going to remain neutral on Circle shares. The latest news could turn out to be a major negative, but nothing is finalized just yet. On the flip side, USDC in circulation has rebounded from the early 2026 lows, and a lack of Fed rate cuts is good for the company's interest generation on reserves. I do think Q1 estimates still remain a little high, but at the moment the potential legislative roadblock could turn out to be a much larger issue than a single quarter's results.

The focus of the portfolio is on balance sheet strength, profitability, earnings visibility and value.

Market overview

Global equity markets ended the year on a positive note, demonstrating resilience amid slowing but still positive economic growth, easing inflation pressures and continued geopolitical uncertainty. Investor sentiment improved, as major central banks shifted further toward a more accommodative policy stance, supporting equity valuations and contributing to lower market volatility. Developed markets outperformed emerging markets during the quarter, with U.S. equities benefiting from resilient corporate earnings, broadening market participation beyond mega cap technology and sustained investment linked to artificial intelligence (AI). European equities advanced on signs of economic stabilization and increased fiscal support. Performance across Asia and emerging markets was mixed, reflecting divergent policy paths, currency headwinds and uneven growth momentum. Overall, equity prices continued to climb, underpinned by resilient fundamentals and expectations of a measured monetary easing cycle into 2026.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future bond holdings are subject to risk.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Our relative underperformance is mainly explained by stock selection in the eurozone and in Norway.

In Europe, stock selection in the eurozone detracted. In the Netherlands, Wolters Kluwer underperformed as investors increasingly assessed the potential impact of AI on the information services sector. Attention was focused on its UpToDate clinical-decision-support platform, which represents a meaningful portion of group revenues, following the emergence of AI-based tools such as OpenEvidence that have gained rapid adoption in the U.S. While the competitive landscape is evolving, we believe Wolters Kluwer's long-term position is underpinned by the depth and reliability of its content, longstanding customer relationships and the integration of its products into hospital systems and clinical workflows. The implications of AI for the business model remain an area of investor focus and are likely to stay under scrutiny through 2026. In France, Air Liquide and Dassault Systemes underperformed. Air Liquide's high-profile Baytown blue ammonia project was frozen indefinitely, with Exxon flagging a lack of guaranteed buyers. This led to some concerns in the market that the energy transition, a key part of the Air Liquide growth story, could be delayed. Dassault Systemes detracted from performance following a late-period share-price decline, driven by softer sentiment toward software stocks and a moderation in near-term growth guidance — despite the company's strong competitive position, resilient industrial demand and improved long-term visibility from its ongoing cloud transition.

In Germany, shares in Deutsche Post jumped on its quarterly results, which beat consensus profit forecasts. This was primarily driven by better-than-expected performance in the Post and Parcel division. The Express division also saw profits grow, aided by effective cost control and despite a fall in volume due to pricing measures. The reiterated guidance for 2025 was taken positively by the market.

In Norway, stock selection detracted. Telenor's shares were weaker during the quarter, partly reflecting a consolidation after strong previous performance. The company's November capital markets day highlighted a sharper strategic focus on the Nordic region, and there is a growing expectation that Telenor might consider selling assets in Asia. In the Nordic region, Telenor is dependent on regulation. Sweden and Denmark represent two markets where Telenor could potentially consolidate to support a more rational competitive structure over time.

In Switzerland, stock selection added. Roche's outperformance was due to positive headline readouts on two of its important pipeline drugs. Expectations for trial success were low, and as a result consensus has been moving up on the news. There has been some clarity on U.S. drug pricing negotiations and tariffs, which has lifted the sector more generally.

In Australia, stock selection added. Rio Tinto performed strongly, delivering strong gains as commodity prices rose and investors rotated into diversified miners. Its new CEO's focus on portfolio simplification and cost control was well received at its investor day in December. Brambles detracted as its share price derated from earlier highs amid valuation normalization and broader market rotation, despite continued strong fundamentals and capital returns.

In Japan, stock selection added. Sumitomo Rubber contributed as earnings momentum improved into year end and upgraded guidance reinforced confidence in a cyclical recovery, underpinned by pricing discipline and strategic restructuring. Mitsubishi Electric delivered strong earnings that drove a share-price rally toward year-end, as investors reacted positively to improving outlooks in its Infrastructure and Life segments. ABC Mart detracted as its share price derated from earlier highs amid uneven monthly sales trends and margin pressure, despite resilient domestic demand supported by inbound tourism.

On a sector basis, our underweight to and stock selection within financials detracted. Stock selection in information technology and materials also detracted. Our stock selection in communication services and consumer staples added. Sector positioning is a residual of the opportunities we find at the country level.

Outlook

In 2026, investors will continue to grapple with geopolitical tensions, trade disruptions and potential inflation surprises linked to AI-related productivity and energy transitions. Companies with strong balance sheets, recurring revenues and durable competitive advantages remain well-positioned amid macro uncertainty. If inflation continues to trend lower, this will anchor bond yields and support risk assets.

The portfolio remains defensively positioned, with an underweight to the quantitative-easing-fueled markets and an overweight to the Southeast Asian markets. The European portfolio is concentrated in the U.K., Switzerland and the relatively healthy economies of core Europe. Economies in Southeast Asia offer sustainable economic growth, supported by increased labor output or productivity growth and trading at more reasonable valuations. We remain underweight to Japan, as its potential economic growth rate remains low, given its poor demographics and low productivity growth.

In terms of sectors, we have limited exposure to more-cyclical sectors such as financials and consumer discretionary. Overweight positions are held in defensive sectors such as industrials and communication services. These sectors offer predictable revenue streams and attractive valuations. The focus of the portfolio is on balance-sheet strength, profitability, earnings visibility and value.

As of period-end on December 31, 2025, the portfolio had no currency hedges in place.

data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Yields shifted

Short and intermediate-maturity Treasury yields fell during the quarter. One- to five-year maturities fell between 0.01 and 0.20%, while longer maturities were relatively stable to slightly higher, resulting in steepening of the overall yield curve.

2 Inflation pressures persisted

Economic data, while limited by the US government shutdown, showed sticky inflation, with tariff-related cost pressures adding further uncertainty. These dynamics in our view increase the likelihood that the US Federal Reserve will keep monetary policy tight for an extended period.

3 Agency MBS benefited from yield curve steepening

US Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) had a positive return for the quarter. High coupon MBS outperformed as implied volatility declined and the yield curve steepened, which supported market segments delivering higher income.

Investment objective

The fund seeks to provide a high level of current income, with liquidity and safety of principal.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 509.90

Portfolio managers

Brian P. Norris, Clint W. Dudley, David Lyle

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Structured credit sectors outperformed Treasuries and investment-grade corporate bonds this quarter.
  • We favor a neutral allocation to Agency MBS because after strong performance in the third quarter, we believe valuations now appear fair relative to historical levels. Technical conditions (supply/demand balance) in our view remain supportive, but we believe the overall risk/reward profile is more balanced.

Portfolio characteristics*

Average duration (years) 5.32
Weighted average life (years) 6.74
Average weighted coupon (%) 3.26
30-day SEC yield (Class A shares) 2.92
30-day SEC unsubsidized yield (Class A shares) -

Portfolio positioning

We maintain a neutral allocation to Agency MBS, reflecting in our view fair valuations after strong recent performance.

Among 30-year mortgages, the fund remains underweight in mortgages with 1.5% - 2.0% coupons, overweight in mortgages with 2.5% - 3.0% coupons and has a neutral weight across mortgages with 3.5% - 6.5% coupons. Among 15-year mortgages, the fund is underweight in mortgages with 1.5% - 2.5% coupons and overweight in mortgages with 4.5% - 5.0% coupons.

The fund's exposure to securities not held in the benchmark continues to focus on selective opportunities across non-Agency MBS, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and asset-backed securities (ABS).

data.title: ACWX: International Stocks May Benefit From Iran De-Escalation
data.description:
Introduction

Global equity markets have seen significant swings in recent weeks amid energy price volatility. While uncertainty is likely to remain high amid the ongoing war in Iran, a potential ceasefire may result in the outperformance of ETFs primarily invested in companies operating in net energy-importing countries.

Investors optimistic about a quick resolution to Middle East hostilities may want to consider the iShares MSCI ACWI ex US ETF (ACWX), which I rate with a Buy rating. Key sticking points behind my bullish outlook include:

  • An attractive 2.79% dividend yield achieved with a modest 0.54x payout ratio, notwithstanding the ETF's cyclical sector tilt.
  • A total return outlook of about 8.94%, with earnings growth driven by retained earnings and the ETF's small allocation to fast-growing emerging markets.
  • Significant diversification across individual holdings and countries, presenting less company-specific risks relative to the S&P 500.

Strategy and Key Characteristics

ACWX is a passive ETF investing in large- and mid-cap companies outside the United States (its benchmark is the MSCI ACWI ex-US index). ACWX invests in both developed and emerging markets, presenting a mix between institutional stability offered by developed markets (which account for the bulk of the ETF's investments), as well as higher GDP growth potential coming from emerging markets.

The portfolio is quite diversified, spanning some 1,727 individual companies, with allocation to the top ten holdings standing at about 14%. This is notably below the circa 37% weight the S&P 500 has in its top ten holdings, indicating that ACWI returns are much less dependent on individual company performance.

From a geographic perspective, allocation is primarily to energy-importing countries in Asia (Japan, China, and Taiwan) and Europe (United Kingdom, France, and Switzerland), although ACWX also has a circa 14% exposure to relative beneficiaries of the current energy crisis, such as Canada, Australia, and Brazil. As such, I believe that any potential de-escalation in Middle East hostilities will be a net benefit for ACWX.

The ETF's expense ratio stands at 0.32%, somewhat above the 0.1-0.2% I typically recommend for passive ETF holdings, but overall acceptable for buy-and-hold long-term investors.

Valuation and Sector Allocation

I calculate ACWX holdings currently trade at a trailing P/E of 17.4x (earnings yield of 5.75%). This remains notably more attractive relative to the S&P 500, where the trailing P/E stands at about 26.1x (earnings yield of 3.83%). As such, we see that current market pricing suggests that S&P 500 earnings would have to grow approximately 1.92% faster in perpetuity just to make up for higher starting valuations. I think this is somewhat unlikely, potentially indicating relative undervaluation of ACWX holdings.

Of course, expectations for near-term ACWX earnings growth are quite muted, as companies in energy-importing countries grapple with high fuel costs and a lack of supplies. Over the medium to long term, however, I am confident that economies will adapt to the new realities. What is more, a de-escalation in the Iran war may allow for a quick recovery.

Near-term headwinds aside, another key reason why ACWX holdings trade at lower valuations is that the ETF's allocation to cyclical sectors such as financials, industrials, consumer discretionary, materials, and energy is quite high, at around 60%. This makes ACWX earnings more volatile depending on the economic cycle, thus justifying a lower P/E multiple.

The ETF's allocation to the information technology sector is only 16.24%, roughly half that of the S&P 500, indicating smaller benefits from AI-related earnings growth for ACWX holdings.

To conclude, while war in the Middle East presents a near-term uncertainty that is reflected in lower valuations for ACWX holdings, I think that the ETF's current valuation provides an attractive entry point for long-term investors. In essence, my thinking is that as the war ends, or alternatively, companies adapt to the new reality, the valuation discount relative to the S&P 500 will begin to shrink.

Return Outlook and Rating

In light of attractive current valuations and a cyclical tilt, it is no surprise that ACWX offers a rather high dividend yield of 2.79%, with growth over the past decade standing at 6.66% per year. Factoring in the 0.32% expense ratio, I calculate that ACWX's payout ratio stands at 0.54x. As such, I think the ETF's distributions are relatively safe even if we see no quick de-escalation in the Iran war.

I arrive at the 0.54x payout ratio by using the 2.79% dividend yield, adding the 0.32% expense ratio to it, and dividing the sum by the earnings yield (5.75%) ACWX holdings trade at. In numbers, we get:

2.79%+0.32%=3.11% for the adjusted yield,

3.11%/5.75%=0.54x payout ratio

I think adding the expense ratio is pertinent as it shows ACWX's ability to pay out dividends after accounting for the expenses the ETF incurs. I opted to use the earnings yield in the calculation since it is updated on a daily basis on the iShares website, akin to the dividend yield. In essence, investors are buying a portfolio of stocks which has produced an earnings yield of 5.75% over the past twelve months, and this portfolio of stocks supports a 2.79% dividend distribution after expenses.

Turning to the benefit from retained earnings, I estimate ACWX holdings should see a circa 2.65% boost to earnings from reinvested profits alone. I view this estimate as quite conservative, since return on invested capital would typically be higher than the one implied in the earnings yield ACWX holdings trade at.

I arrive at the 2.65% retained earnings contribution by multiplying the retention ratio of 0.46x (1.00 minus the 0.54x payout ratio above) by the 5.75% earnings yield. In numbers, we get

0.46*5.75%=2.26% contribution from retained earnings

The third component driving ACWX returns will naturally be nominal economic growth in countries where ACWX holdings operate. While a prolonged war in the Middle East will likely result in a low-growth, high-inflation environment in the near term, over the long term I do expect ACWX holdings to benefit from nominal GDP growth of about 3.5%, as slower growth in Japan and Europe is somewhat offset by stronger GDP growth dynamics in emerging markets, Canada, and Australia. My calculations are summarized in the table below:

Return Component Contribution Commentary
Dividend 2.79% Relatively safe thanks to a low 0.54x payout ratio, notwithstanding high cyclical exposure.
Retained Earnings 2.65% Conservative estimate as return on invested capital likely above earnings yield.
Nominal GDP Growth 3.5% Too optimistic in the near term given the Middle East war, but overall achievable in the long term.
Total 8.94% Attractive relative to the S&P 500

Source: Author calculations

As you can tell from the table above, my calculations show a high single-digit total return outlook of about 8.94% for ACWX investors, which I see as quite attractive relative to the S&P 500. Hence I believe that a Buy rating is warranted for ACWX, notwithstanding risks, which I will discuss in the next section.

Risks

In the near term, a principal risk to consider is the ongoing war in the Middle East and its effect on cyclical company earnings, which account for roughly 60% of ACWX holdings. A prolonged conflict will likely result in a stagflationary environment, potentially resulting in ACWX undershooting my long-term return outlook of 8.94%.

While the allocation to emerging markets (circa 15% of ETF assets) such as China, India, and Brazil naturally results in a higher total return outlook thanks to their robust GDP growth dynamics, investors should note risks stemming from weaker corporate governance practices and a somewhat smaller link between emerging markets GDP growth and corporate earnings growth. As such, the anticipated 3.5% nominal earnings boost from economy-wide GDP growth may be somewhat optimistic.

A weaker U.S. dollar (DXY) was a tailwind for ACWX returns in 2025. That said, a reversal of dollar weakness may result in ACWX underperforming the S&P 500 in USD terms. This is due to the fact that the ETF employs no currency hedging, with its net asset value dependent on the prices of stocks traded around the world in various local currencies such as the EUR or JPY.

Conclusion

Despite war in the Middle East, the ACWX ETF has marginally outperformed the S&P 500 so far in 2026. This comes as international stocks remain notably cheaper relative to large-cap U.S. peers, providing a margin of safety for investors even as the earnings growth outlook is blurred in the near term.

I calculate ACWX should deliver a high single-digit total return over the medium to long term, with roughly a third of returns coming in the form of dividends. This should be enough for ACWX to outperform the S&P 500, warranting a Buy rating on the ETF.

Key risks in the investment case include ACWX's outsized allocation to cyclical sectors, which may backfire if the war in the Middle East drags on; the ETF's emerging markets exposure, as well as a potential reversal of recent U.S. dollar weakness.

Thank you for reading.
